coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots for enhanced oil recovery in carbonate reservoirs
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Sakthivel, Sivabalan
Zhou, Xianmin
Giannelis, Emmanuel P.
Kanj, Mazen Y.
coredata.dc:description:
Enhanced oil recovery (EOR) operations in oil-wet carbonate reservoirs face enormous challenges such as long-term chemical and thermal stability issues, high retention and adsorption, diffusion, and, accordingly, an inability to reach deep into the reservoir. This study investigates the EOR potential of Carbon nanodots (CNDs) in coreflood experiments using both outcrop and reservoir carbonate samples. CNDs have excellent colloidal stability and mobility in the harsh carbonate reservoir environment. We also compared its performance against an in-house Gemini surfactant (GS8) of excellent surfactancy properties. Experiments were performed at the ”typical” Saudi Arabian reservoir conditions. We tested three flooding patterns using different rock permeabilities and additive concentrations in the SW. Based on the results, the added oil recovery with CNDs and GS8 ranged from 3%–23% and 6%–22%, respectively. At the same dilute concentration, CNDs are more effective than GS8 in increasing oil recovery. X-ray coreflood experiments provided a real-time 1-D saturation profile. It allowed gauging the efficacy of each additive in situ while tracking the oil and water saturation in and recovery from the carbonate samples. The saturation profiles were compared qualitatively against the oil recovery data from these experiments. Unsteady state, two-phase relative permeability curves of the X-ray coreflood experiments revealed the flow characteristics of oil and water during the injection of different fluids. Both CNDs and GS8 increased the oil’s relative permeability,
k
ro
and reduced the water’s relative permeability,
k
rw
. Overall, CNDs proved highly efficient, economical, and more scalable than a leading surfactant candidate. CNDs are based on a simple, single-pot synthesis process and have a proven-stability and mobility in the field. And in dilute concentrations (10–200 ppm), it alters the dynamic wettability of the carbonate reservoir favorably. Potentially, these can boost the oil recovery at depths (from the injector) that are unreachable by the conventional surfactants.

Tests are run at reservoir conditions (100 °C temperature and 4500 psi confining pressure).

[GS8] in Stage 3 is maintained at 200 ppm. Tests are run at reservoir conditions (100 °C temperature and 4500 psi confining pressure).

Coreflood oil recovery performances for the SW-GS8-CND flood pattern on the S110 (110 mD) outcrop sample as a function of injected PV. [GS8] in Stage 2 is maintained at 200 ppm. [CND] in Stage 3 varies from 10 to 5000 ppm. Tests are run at reservoir conditions (100 °C temperature and 4500 psi confining pressure).

Coreflood oil recovery performances for the SW-{CND,GS8} flood pattern on the outcrop samples S060 (60 mD) and S110 (110 mD) as a function of injected PV. In the second and final stage of this pattern, the concentration of each additive in the injected SW is maintained at 200 ppm. Tests are run at reservoir conditions (100 °C temperature and 4500 psi confining pressure).

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots for enhanced oil recovery in carbonate reservoirs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enhanced oil recovery (EOR) operations in oil-wet carbonate reservoirs face enormous challenges such as long-term chemical and thermal stability issues, high retention and adsorption, diffusion, and, accordingly, an inability to reach deep into the reservoir. This study investigates the EOR potential of Carbon nanodots (CNDs) in coreflood experiments using both outcrop and reservoir carbonate samples. CNDs have excellent colloidal stability and mobility in the harsh carbonate reservoir environment. We also compared its performance against an in-house Gemini surfactant (GS8) of excellent surfactancy properties. Experiments were performed at the ”typical” Saudi Arabian reservoir conditions.

We tested three flooding patterns using different rock permeabilities and additive concentrations in the SW. Based on the results, the added oil recovery with CNDs and GS8 ranged from 3%–23% and 6%–22%, respectively. At the same dilute concentration, CNDs are more effective than GS8 in increasing oil recovery. X-ray coreflood experiments provided a real-time 1-D saturation profile. It allowed gauging the efficacy of each additive in situ while tracking the oil and water saturation in and recovery from the carbonate samples. The saturation profiles were compared qualitatively against the oil recovery data from these experiments. Unsteady state, two-phase relative permeability curves of the X-ray coreflood experiments revealed the flow characteristics of oil and water during the injection of different fluids. Both CNDs and GS8 increased the oil’s relative permeability,
k
ro
and reduced the water’s relative permeability,
k
rw
.

Overall, CNDs proved highly efficient, economical, and more scalable than a leading surfactant candidate. CNDs are based on a simple, single-pot synthesis process and have a proven-stability and mobility in the field. And in dilute concentrations (10–200 ppm), it alters the dynamic wettability of the carbonate reservoir favorably. Potentially, these can boost the oil recovery at depths (from the injector) that are unreachable by the conventional surfactants.

ce:section.ce:para:
It is estimated that up to 55% of the world’s reservoirs are naturally fractured oil-wet carbonate rock formations. Moreover, the primary and secondary recovery processes in these reservoirs contribute to a mere 35%–45% of the original oil in place (OOIP). During waterflooding, the water cannot displace all the oil in the matrix due to oil trapping by capillary forces. As such, huge amounts of the oil remain unrecovered as residual, trapped, or bypassed. High capillary pressure of trapped oil, unfavorable wettability, and high oil–water interfacial tension lead to early water breakthrough and poor sweep efficiency (Nilsson et al., 1997). The microscopic displacement of the trapped oils can be achieved by either decreasing the oil phase pressure (
P
nw
) or increasing the water phase pressure (
P
w
). Only then does the water phase flow more easily under capillary or imbibition (Li et al., 2009; McPhee et al., 2015). Chemical EOR (CEOR) is one of the most suited EOR methods for the Saudi Arabian reservoir environment. It targets the fluid–fluid and fluid–rock interactions in the reservoir. The preferential wetting phase (e.g., surfactants, nanoparticles, etc.) invades the rock and forces the trapped oil (non-wetting phase) displacement. The essence of the process is that a pressure difference, Pc, between the two phases is developed by increasing capillary pressure in the displacing fluid (Li et al., 2009). However, wettability alteration (rock–fluid interaction) and IFT reduction (fluid–fluid interaction) depend on many parameters. These include, among others, rock type (mineralogy), pH, crude oil composition, ionic composition and strength of the connate and injection brines, exposure time, and reservoir temperature and pressure (Kanj et al., 2020).

Saudi Arabian carbonate reservoirs are characterized by high temperature and high salinity conditions. The average temperature in the reservoir is close to 100 °C. Both connate formation water (FW) and injection seawater (SW) have complex salinities with high amounts of divalent ions. The total dissolved solids (TDS) value in the FW and the SW is equivalent to 240,000 ppm and 67,500 ppm, respectively (Kanj et al., 2020). Off-the-shelf surfactants, such as olefin sulfonates, alkyl ether sulfates, and alkylbenzene sulfonates, possess poor solubility in high salinity brines and poor chemical stability at high temperatures (exceeding 90 °C). Additionally, most oilfield chemicals are prone to adsorption in the rock matrix. Issues such as biocompatibility, degradation, precipitation, retention, and adsorption are key contributors to oilfield chemicals loss and formation damage in the reservoir during an ill-suited CEOR treatment. This limits the CEOR treatment to closely spaced pilot wells, as deep-reservoir EOR remains out of reach.

Many investigated the use of inorganic (e.g., silicon oxide, titanium oxide, aluminum oxide, zirconium oxide, magnesium oxide, iron oxide) and organic (e.g., graphene particles, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes) nanoparticles on wettability alteration of carbonate formation. They assessed the nanoparticles’ performance on the oil recovery via spontaneous imbibition or coreflood testing and demonstrated only varying levels of success and limited to no scalability and oilfield use potential. Recovery depends on the dynamic rock–fluid interaction before and during the nanofluid injection. It also depends on the ability of the nanofluid to alter wettability from its native, oil-wet state and improve the relative permeability of the oil. Karimi et al. (2012) investigated the rock–water–air contact angle at atmospheric conditions, with and without ZrO2 (24 nm) in deionized water. They observed the formation of a nanometer-sized ribbon and flower-like morphology layers on the carbonate sample while aging the sample in the nanofluid solution, which indicates the adsorption of particles on the rock surface (Karimi et al., 2012). Bayat et al. (2014) studied the metal oxide (Al2O3, TiO2, and SiO2) nanoparticles at 26–60 °C for static wettability alteration of the carbonate rock–oil–water system (Bayat et al., 2014). They noted that the adsorption of nanoparticles on the rock surface altered the wettability of the limestone. Mohammadi et al. (2014) explored the application of gamma-Al2O3 (0.5 wt% in DI water) for wettability alteration and oil recovery using a carbonate reservoir sample from an Iranian field. They ran a coreflood experiment at room temperature and 5500 psi confining pressure and observed 11.25% of added oil recovery (Mohammadi et al., 2014). Roustaei and Bagherzadeh (2015) employed 20−70 nm hydrophilic SiO2 nanoparticles (4 g/L or 4000 ppm) in 5% NaCl in a coreflood experiment and estimated a 10% increased recovery. The experiment was conducted under ambient conditions (Roustaei and Bagherzadeh, 2015). Moradi et al. (2015) ran water-alternating-gas (WAG) CO2 injection in a coreflood setup with and without SiO2 nanoparticles (12 nm and 35 nm) at 50 °C and 800 psi. They estimated 22% of increased recovery with the smaller-sized particles due to their increased ability to alter the rock wettability by adsorbing onto the rock surface (Moradi et al., 2015). Kuang et al. (2018) tested (15–20 nm) SiO2 nanoparticles at 0.1 wt% concentration in a brine solution (1 mM NaCl) on Edwards limestone carbonate samples in a coreflood setup at 60 °C and 1000 psi. They reported a mere 4% increase in recovery over neat brine flooding. They also reported stability issues of the nanoparticles when used in conjunction with a nonionic surfactant (Kuang et al., 2018). AfzaliTabar et al. (2020) explored nanoemulsion formulation using N-doped graphene and MoS2 quantum dots nanohybrids for IFT reduction, wettability alteration, and EOR. They conducted coreflood experiments at ambient conditions in temperature and reported close to 22% of added recovery with just 0.01% concentration of quantum dots in DI water (AfzaliTabar et al., 2020).

In all these studies, several mechanisms were reported to justify the improved recovery with the nanofluid. These include wettability alteration (Karimi et al., 2012; Bayat et al., 2014; Roustaei and Bagherzadeh, 2015; Moradi et al., 2015; Kuang et al., 2018; Sharma and Mohanty, 2013), IFT reduction (AfzaliTabar et al., 2020; Peng et al., 2017), nanoemulsion formation (AfzaliTabar et al., 2020), structural disjoining and wedging effects (Kanj et al., 2020), and oil viscosity reduction (Peng et al., 2017). By far, the most prevalent of these is the wettability alteration mechanism. Wasan and Nikolov (2003), Hammond and Unsal (2009, 2011), Kondiparty et al. (2011, 2012), Liu et al. (2012), Zhang et al. (2014) examined the imbibing characteristics of nanofluids at the three-phase contact lines of rock–oil–water. They attributed the dynamic wettability alteration to an increased capillarity of the nanofluid resulting in the reduction of the adhesion characteristics on the rock surface and the expulsion of the oil from the pores (Hammond and Unsal, 2009, 2011; Kondiparty et al., 2011, 2012; Liu et al., 2012; Wasan and Nikolov, 2003; Zhang et al., 2014).

Despite their impact on wettability, the majority of nanoparticles used in the literature for EOR remain untested for colloidal stability (and mobility) in the field at reservoir conditions. Some reported instability issues in the experiments at higher salt concentrations and high temperatures leading to pore plugging and formation damage. Injection seawater and connate water have high ionic strength and high ionic complexity with large amounts of divalent ions, such as Ca2＋ and Mg
2 +
. Yet, all the studies listed above used only simple salts with monovalent ions (e.g., NaCl). Moreover, the synthesis of nanoparticles used in these studies often lacks simplicity and the potential for scalability. For example, their synthesis involves several steps and expensive precursors or requires complex functional additives. And the nanoparticles are employed in large concentrations, making it uneconomical for field use and application.

We field-tested the 3−8 nm Carbon nanodots (CNDs) for tracer application in the Saudi Arabian oilfields (Kanj and Kosynkin, 2015). We also studied the CNDs use for foam stability and dynamic wettability alterations (Kanj et al., 2020; Sakthivel et al., 2019). CNDs are typically composed of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. Their synthesis involves a one-pot process that is scalable to tonnes quantity. The CNDs are highly fluorescent and detectable to ppb levels. And they have been demonstrated to possess high chemical, thermal, and colloidal stability (Kanj and Kosynkin, 2015; Krysmann et al., 2012).

In this study, we explored using CNDs for EOR to gain further insights into its oil displacement efficiency and its contribution to added oil recovery in dilute concentrations. We benchmarked their performance against a well-tested in-house Gemini surfactant, GS8, with exceptional surfactancy properties (Hussain et al., 2019). We ran an extensive experimental study using both outcrop and reservoir carbonate samples. Outcrop samples provided a platform with some controlled properties for early screening and testing. These allowed extrapolating on the performance beyond what we could achieve with the limited selection of reservoir samples at hand. Tests were run at reservoir conditions (100 °C for temperature, 4500 psi for overburden pressure, and 3200 psi for pore pressure) using filtered crude oil, synthetic seawater, and synthetic formation water. Our parametric study involved testing core samples with different air permeabilities (15–516 mD), varying the CNDs concentration in the SW (10–5000 ppm), and manipulating the order of the injection pattern of the different fluids (neat SW, CNDs in SW, GS8 in SW, and a mixture of CNDs and GS8 in SW). Additionally, we employed an X-ray coreflood setup to monitor the saturation profile and analyze the end effects on long outcrop samples (1.5 ”
× 6 ” ). Subsequently, the unsteady state, two-phase relative permeability curves for these tests were extracted to interpret the dominant mechanisms in SW, CNDs in SW, and GS8 in SW floods. Lastly, dynamic wettability measurements contributed to the understanding of the mechanisms of oil recovery using the CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials: Crude oil, nanodots, surfactants, brines, and rock samples
ce:section.ce:para: We used dead crude oil from a Saudi Arabian reservoir for all the experiments in this study. The output from the SARA (saturates, aromatics, resins, and asphaltenes) analysis of the oil yielded the oil composition and properties. The oil has a significant amount of aromatics (50%) and saturates (36.2%), and a good amount of resins (11%) and asphaltenes (2.8%). The oil’s viscosity, density, and API gravity were 15.1 cP, 872.4 kg/m3, and 28.75, respectively. On the other hand, the oil acid number is high at 6.2 mg KOH/g.

The Carbon nanodots, CNDs (Sakthivel et al., 2019; Krysmann et al., 2012) and Gemini surfactant-8, GS8 (Hussain et al., 2019) were synthesized as described in the literature. The colloidal stability of the CNDs was validated in the lab at an extreme environment in temperature (exceeding 150 °C) and salinity (TDS up to 240,000 ppm), surpassing that of the targeted reservoir application (Kanj et al., 2009). These were also tested in the field with great success for stability, and they showed no retention, scalability, and mobility (Kanj and Kosynkin, 2015; Kanj et al., 2011). The characterization of the CNDs and their physicochemical properties are presented in the supplementary materials (Figures S1–S3 and Table S1). Our in-house GS8 was adopted for benchmarking purposes because of its demonstrated efficacy and performance. GS-8 outperforms most off-the-shelf surfactants. We employed CNDs in SW at concentrations of 10, 50, 200, 2500, and 5000 ppm. The concentration of GS8 in SW was maintained at 200 ppm, well above the CMC ( < 150 ppm) (Hussain et al., 2019). Since we targeted using only dilute concentrations of CNDs, we accordingly limited the concentration of GS8 to 200 ppm for comparison. Table 1 presents the ionic composition and strength of the injection SW and the connate formation water for easy reference.

Conventional HP/HT coreflood experiments employed Indiana limestone outcrop (1.5 ”
× 2.9 ” ) and Saudi Arabian reservoir (1.5 ”
× 2.5 ” ) core plugs. These helped demonstrate the CNDs’ standardized and reservoir-specific EOR efficacy. It also helped generate a better understanding of the recovery variation between the two rock systems. The X-ray coreflood setup accommodates only long plugs to minimize the end effects on the saturation profile. Experiments employing this setup were done using an outcrop sample, 1.5 ” in diameter and 6 ” long.

Both outcrop and reservoir disc-shaped (1 ”
× 9/64 ” ) samples were used to perform the static and dynamic contact angle (wettability) measurements on the rock–oil–brine system with and without CNDs. The petrophysical properties of all disc samples used for wettability measurements and core samples used for coreflood testing are presented in Table 2.

Initially, all the core samples were cleaned thoroughly by the Soxhlet extraction method (Dean-Stark apparatus) using toluene and methanol and dried at 100 °C for 1–2 days. Then, the petrophysical properties of the samples, such as porosity and permeability, were analyzed using an Automated Helium Porosimeter and Permeameter (APP-608) based on Boyle’s law and the steady-state method.

All core plug samples were treated in vacuum at −14 psi, saturated with the high salinity FW (TDS = 240,000 ppm), and pressurized to 3000 psi for 2–4 h to ensure fluid access to all pores. Samples were then placed in the capillary pressure desaturation setup (pore-plate method). The setup was then pressurized step-wise to 175 psi using
N
2
gas, and the system was shut for 3–5 days to achieve the irreducible water saturation,
S
wi
. Afterwards, the samples were degassed under vacuum and saturated with oil using a coreflood setup. The system parameters included 4500 psi confining pressure, 3200 psi back-pressure, 0.1 cc/min injection rate, and room temperature. Later, the oil-saturated samples were soaked in the cured oil container and aged for two weeks at 100 °C and ambient pressure.

Fourteen disc-shaped rock samples were prepared for the wettability measurement. All the samples were cleaned using Soxhlet extraction, as stated before. Half of the samples were saturated directly (at zero
S
wi
) in oil under vacuum, pressurized to 3000 psi, and aged for two weeks at 100 °C and atmospheric pressure. The rest of the samples followed the same procedure described above for the core plugs for saturating with oil (under vacuum) at
S
wi
and aging for two weeks at 100 °C.

Fig. 1 presents a schematic of the X-ray (relative permeability apparatus) coreflood setup. The conventional coreflood system has the same design, but it lacks the CT Scanner component and substitutes the carbon fiber core holder with a stainless steel core holder. After aging, the oil-saturated samples are loaded into the core holder and pressurized to 4500 psi with 3200 psi of back-pressure. The system temperature is set at 100 °C. Testing starts by measuring the oil permeability of the sample at
S
wi
. The pressure drop across the core sample at the steady-state response was measured at different flow rates (e.g., 0.1, 0.25, and 0.5 cc/min). Thereafter, the lines are cleaned, and neat SW is injected at 0.1 cc/min. At the maximum water cut, we switch to the next fluid in the sequence and maintain the same injection rate. The oil comes out as an emulsion. This was demulsified either by heating to 70–90° or via adding toluene. Separated oil was recorded with an accuracy of ± 0.05 mL. All injection fluids (SW, CND in SW, GS8 in SW, and CND+GS8 in SW) registered comparable viscosities (Table S2) and resulted in similar pressure gradients at the constant rate of 0.1 cc/min.

The flooding sequence SW-CND-GS8, for example, denotes injecting SW, CNDs in SW, and GS8 in SW, sequentially in the sample. We switch fluid only after a maximum water cut or when a steady-state response is achieved. The two flooding sequences SW-CND-GS8 and SW-GS8-CND, helped gauge the efficacy of each additive in SW and its contribution to the overall recovery. The flooding pattern SW-{CND,GS8}, where a mixture of CND and GS8 in SW is employed in the second and final stage, helped reveal the level of synergism or competitiveness between the two additives on recovery. Table 3 summarizes all the coreflood experiments of this study with recovery data. The reproducibility of the results was verified in all of these tests with an error value of ± 1% or less. For example, the reproducibility was also inherent in the pattern SW-GS8-CND for the first two stages on the same samples. These should remain practically unchanged from one test to the next. Moreover, many tests were performed sequentially on the same sample (e.g., Tests #7–17 in Table 3). After each test, the sample was thoroughly cleaned using the Soxhlet extraction method in preparation for the next coreflood test.

The X-ray saturation monitoring system (Fig. 1) was employed to accurately measure the fluids distribution profile in-situ in the sample during the coreflood experiment. Different fluids in the sample absorb the X-rays differently. The difference between the applied and the detected X-ray beams is used to quantify the oil or the water saturation profile. We used Sodium Iodide (NaI) as a tracer in the SW to improve its contrast against the crude oil. As such, we modified the salinity composition of both SW and FW, as shown in Table 4. We employed the same amount of NaI as NaCl in the FW but almost quadrupled it in the SW. The X-ray system allowed the simultaneous in-situ measurement of both the residual oil and the water saturation profiles and the derivation of the relative permeability curves in the same test. This allowed for a qualitative assessment of the individual performance of the additives in the SW on recovery. Tests #39–41 of Table 3 intended to monitor the saturation profile in-situ and analyze the end effects with different additives in the SW. The X-ray coreflood testing cannot be directly correlated with conventional coreflood experiments (e.g., Tests #3, #6, #9, #14, or #17) due to variations in the SW composition and the aging process adopted between these two types of experiments.

Samples are scanned from the injection (upstream) point to the production (downstream) point to produce a one-dimensional saturation profile. The system is calibrated using aluminum blocks (cubes) before doing any experimental scan. The edge length of the aluminum blocks was selected to yield the same attenuations as the dry rock samples. System calibration is performed prior to every scan to monitor if there are any external fluctuations. The energy level to generate the X-ray was set and maintained as 54.2 kV and 30 mA (or 1626 W) throughout the study.

Initially, FW-saturated samples were loaded into the Carbon fiber core holder and subjected to 4500 psi and 3200 psi of overburden pressure and back-pressure, respectively. Tests were run at 100 °C. Scanning was performed on the sample at a constant gap of 0.2 mm and with a continuous injection of the FW at 0.1 cc/min. Once a stable saturation profile is attained, oil saturation was initiated. This is followed by brine desaturation to
S
wi.
At
S
wi
, the sample was scanned, and the X-ray intensity was measured. The oil injection continued for 24 h (at a rate of 0.5 cc/min), and the intensity was re-measured for confirmation. X-ray coreflood monitoring experiments involved three types of pattern injection: (i) SW-CND-GS8, (ii) SW-GS8-CND, and (iii) CND. The concentration of both CNDs and GS8 in the SW was kept constant at 200 ppm, and the injection rate was maintained at 0.1 cc/min in these tests.

The relative permeability of a fluid (either oil or water) measures the rate of the fluid phase mobility in the porous media as a function of the applied pressure gradient and saturation. It is an essential industry parameter for predicting reservoir wettability, oil recovery performance, and productivity. In this broad context, we characterized the two-phase unsteady-state relative permeability for all three pattern injection types (i.e., SW-CND-GS8, SW-GS8-CND, and direct CND) using the X-ray coreflood system.

The Corey model (Corey, 1954) is widely accepted for its simplicity and better accuracy in the consolidated porous media (Honarpour et al., 2018). The model uses the power-law approximations of Eqs. (3) and (4) to estimate the relative permeability characteristics.

An interfacial tensiometer (IFT-700 from Vinci Tech) was employed to measure the static and dynamic rock–oil–brine contact angles (invert sessile drop method) and the oil–brine​ interfacial tension (pendant drop method) as per Kanj et al. (2020). Both outcrop (110 mD) and reservoir (120 mD) disc-like samples were used in these experiments. In the dynamic contact angle measurements, GS8 caused an instantaneous collapse of the oil droplet by rapid reduction of interfacial tension. Accordingly, it was excluded from these measurements. All IFT and wettability measurements were performed at 100 °C and atmospheric pressure.

In general, the efficiency of an additive in displacing oil in a porous media decreases with increased recovery or reduced oil content in the rock matrix. This efficiency requires optimizing for increased recovery (% OOIP) and reduced treatment time (injected pore volume, PV) and investment (cost of additive per injected unit volume of SW). Kanj and Kosynkin (2015) demonstrated the CNDs scalability to metric tonne levels due to the simplicity of the synthetic approach (one pot and single-step synthesis) at minimal costs (Kanj and Kosynkin, 2015). The CNDs can be readily mixed with SW using in-line mixers for continuous injection at dilute concentrations. The two remaining objectives in this optimization process can be realized by running coreflood patterns following neat SW injection that consider the efficacy of both additives taken separately (e.g., SW-CND or SW-GS8), sequentially (e.g., SW-CND-GS8 or SW-GS8-CND), and jointly (e.g., SW-{CND,GS8}) on the oil recovery as a function of the concentration of additives in the SW. A wide range of permeability values from outcrop and reservoir samples were considered here. Two outcrop samples with air permeabilities values of 60 mD (S060) and 110 mD (S110) and three reservoir samples with air permeability values of 15 mD (R015), 120 mD (R120), and 516 mD (R516) were employed for the coreflooding tests. Coreflood experiments continued until oil production in the effluent drops below 2% for a minimum of 0.5 PV at 0.1 cc/min. For the sake of aligning the plots, the plateaus were trimmed to 3.4 PV, 8.83 PV, and 12.6 PV for Stages 1 (neat SW), 2, and 3 (also case of mixed additives), respectively. Subsequently, we examined the dynamic saturation profiles generated during the X-ray coreflood tests for each of the additives in the SW. X-ray coreflood testing was performed on one long outcrop Indiana limestone sample (S065) of 65 mD air permeability. Finally, we measured both the static and the dynamic rock–oil–brine contact angles for both outcrop and reservoir samples with and without CNDs in the SW. Along with the oil–brine IFT measurements, the contact angle measurements supported the analyses and helped explain observations from the coreflood experiments.

Two Indiana limestone outcrop samples (S060 and S110) were employed in the conventional HP/HT coreflood experiments. Outcrop samples provided a platform with some controlled properties for early screening and testing. S110 has almost double the permeability of S060. Figs. 2 and 3 demonstrate the SW-CND-GS8 sequence for samples S060 and S110, respectively. Fig. 4 shows the SW-GS8-CND pattern for sample S110. We opted to omit the SW-GS8-CND coreflood pattern on S060 as follow-up tests on S110 and all reservoir samples demonstrated little difference in the total recovery between SW-CND-GS8 and SW-GS8-CND. Fig. 5 shows the combined effect of CNDs and GS8 mixture on both samples. In these tests, we employed CNDs in five different concentrations (10, 50, 200, 2500, and 5000 ppm). GS8 was maintained at a fixed concentration of 200 ppm in SW, wherever applicable.

Figs. 2–4 demonstrate the reproducibility in Stage 1 (SW flood) for all tests. The average value and standard deviation pair for the Stage 1 recovery is calculated as (41.54%, 0.49%) and (43.36%, 0.58%) for S060 and S110, respectively. The reproducibility in S110 is extended to Stage 2 for SW-GS8-CND with an average oil recovery increment (ORI) of 15.34%. The standard deviation in the latter case remains low at only 0.56%. The higher permeability sample (S110) presented a faster early recovery rate and a higher plateau (43.36%) than S060. Moreover, the Stage 1 plateau is reached faster in S110 (2PV) than S060 (2–2.5PV). Higher permeability samples have higher connectivity of pores and, accordingly, faster recovery by water flood and possibly higher total recovery. The rate of recovery is sharp at the start of Stage 1, more so for S110 (Fig. 3) than S060 (Fig. 2) as a result of mobilizing the oil in all easily accessible pores. The highest early recovery difference can be estimated at 5%–10% in the neighborhood of the 1PV mark in favor of S110. However, the plateau gap at the end of Stage 1 between the two samples is narrow (1.82%, which is the difference between 43.36% and 41.54%).

ce:para.ce:bold: Additive Performance and Recovery Mechanisms in Stage 2

ce:section.ce:para: Additives in SW in Stage 2 generated higher ORI values for the higher permeability sample, S110. For CNDs in Stage 2, the ORI values obtained for S110 constantly exceeded the ones obtained for S060. Moreover, we noticed that ORI is more sensitive to changes in smaller rather than larger additive concentration values. For the mixed additives case (Fig. 5), the ORI increased from 21.55% to 22.99% in favor of the higher permeability sample. However, mixing additives demonstrated a good improvement over any one additive (in this case, it is only CNDs) alone at the same concentration of 200 ppm in the SW. The Stage 2 ORI increased from 15.68% to 21.55% for S060 and from 17.8% to 22.99% for S110. In Stage 2 of Fig. 3, the onset of recovery is faster, and the rate of recovery thereafter is higher, leading to a higher plateau for a higher concentration of CNDs in SW. Comparing the performance of CNDs in Fig. 3 to GS8 in Fig. 4 in Stage 2 at the same concentration of 200 ppm, GS8 gives a faster onset and a higher rate of recovery than CNDs. However, the total recovery at the end of Stage 2 was 61.30% and 58.76% for CNDs and GS8, respectively, favoring CNDs slightly. The recovery value for GS8 in Stage 2 from Fig. 4 (58.76%) is the mean of all five tests. Accordingly, there is a clear advantage for CNDs over GS8 not only in its ease of synthesis and scalability but also in its improved total recovery efficiency at dilute concentrations below 200 ppm in the SW.

ce:para.ce:bold: Effect of Concentration on the Spread of ORI in Stages 2 and 3

ce:section.ce:para: The discrepancies in the oil recovery increments are larger for smaller concentrations of CNDs in SW. The change is more pronounced at concentrations below 200 ppm than above it. For example, with S060, increasing the concentration of CNDs from 10 ppm to 50 ppm, the oil recovery contribution increased 3.05 units; whereas, increasing this concentration from 2500 ppm to 5000 ppm increased the recovery by only 1.27 units (Table 3 and Fig. 2). The same conclusion can be made in Stage 3 for SW-GS8-CND with S110 (Fig. 4). In the latter case, the respective recovery increase amounts to 3.0 and 1.70 units. This can be explained by considering:

ce:list-item.ce:para: The efficiency of an additive in displacing oil in a porous media decreases with increased recovery or reduced oil content in the rock matrix (Stage 3 of Figs. 2 and 3).

The recovery increases, but the efficiency drops with a higher concentration of the additive in the SW. For example, an increase from 10 ppm to 50 ppm for CNDs in Stage 2 for S110 (Fig. 3) gives 2.78 recovery units increase, while an increase from 10 ppm to 5000 ppm gives only 12.67 recovery units increase (Fig. 3). Overall, we see a nonlinear increase in recovery with the increase in CND concentration. Kondiparty et al. (2012) and Liu et al. (2012) also noted a nonlinear dependence of the contact line displacement with the nanoparticle concentration (Kondiparty et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2012). Particles in the wedge are structured with increased entropy of the system and more freedom in the bulk solution. The freedom is reduced in saturated solutions. Again, this observation is not limited to CNDs or nanoparticles but covers equally oilfield chemicals. In higher concentrations, CNDs and other oilfield chemicals yield more recovery, but their efficiency tends to drop. This is due to, for example, micelle formation, adsorption, aggregations, and poor mobility, among other factors.

For a given concentration of the additive in the SW, the ORI in the current Stage drops as the starting recovery state (plateau from the previous stage) gets higher. In SW-CND-GS8, for example, the ORI in Stage 3 constantly drops as the recovery state (or CNDs concentration in SW) of Stage 2 increases (Figs. 2 and 3).

ce:para.ce:bold: Synergism and Competitiveness between CNDs and GS8

ce:section.ce:para: Kanj et al. (2020) and Sakthivel and Kanj (2021) noted that there is a strong effect for both GS8 and CNDs on dynamic wettability alteration, spontaneous imbibition, and recovery over neat SW. They also noted that while GS8 is more efficient in lowering the IFT, CNDs’ efficiency can be attributed to a strong favorable shift in the dynamic wettability due to capillary pressure reduction and disjoining pressure enhancement (Kanj et al., 2020; Sakthivel and Kanj, 2021). GS8 outperforms CNDs with an early onset of recovery and a shorter reach to plateau. However, CNDs contribute to a higher plateau value or ORI than GS8 at the same dilute concentration of 200 ppm in the SW (Fig. 6). The rapid early effect for GS8 is obviously due to its surfactancy (more effective in lowering IFT) and molecular (more diffusive) nature. Comparing the two plots corresponding to the sequential use of additives in Fig. 6, CNDs outperformed GS8 in oil recovery contribution in Stages 2 and 3.

The results summarized in Table 3 demonstrate that SW-CND-GS8, SW-GS8-CND, and SW-{CND,GS8} give comparable total recoveries for all tests using S110 and the same CNDs concentration (GS8 concentration is maintained at 200 ppm) in the SW. This is also confirmed in Fig. 6. To further validate this response, we conducted dynamic wettability and interfacial studies. It is presented at the end of the Section on Results and Discussions.

Testing on reservoir samples followed the same coreflood protocols and experimental procedures detailed earlier. Here, we tested reservoir samples with three different gas permeability values (15 mD, 120 mD, and 516 mD). Based on the outcomes from the previous analyses on S060 and S110, we opted to limit the CNDs concentration in the SW to 10, 50, and 200 ppm. Wherever applicable, the concentration of GS8 in the SW was maintained at 200 ppm. Fig. 7 shows the oil recovery performances of different flood patterns (CW-CND-GS8, SW-GS8-CND, and SW-{CND,GS8}) as a function of injected fluids, rock permeability, and CNDs concentration. A total of 21 independent coreflood tests are presented in Fig. 7. The vertical dividers in each inset figure identify the stages in each pattern experiment. The same recovery mechanisms and comparative responses that were observed earlier for the outcrop samples apply here to Tests #18-38 of Table 3 with the reservoir samples.

In Fig. 7, the reproducibility of the recovery response for Stage 1 is maintained on the same reservoir sample. The shape and the level of the recovery curve repeat across all seven tests on any one core plug (e.g., see Fig. 7a, d, and g). Moreover, the recovery reach is shorter (i.e., the recovery rate is faster), and the recovery plateau (or ORI) is higher for more permeable samples. The Stage 1 recovery values varied between 42.10% and 42.88% for R516 and between 38.51% and 40.60% for R015. The 7-test average for R015, R120, and R516 is calculated as 39.81%, 41.14%, and 42.46%, respectively.

For SW-GS8-CND patterns, the reproducibility of the recovery response extends to Stage 2 on the same samples. Overall, an increase in the concentration of CNDs in the SW ([CND]) generated higher recovery values in this stage. The Stage 2 ORI in the SW-CND-GS8 pattern flooding increased by 3.71, 4.85, and 5.62 points for R015, R120, and R516, respectively. This is accomplished over the 20 fold increase in [CND] to 200 ppm. The ORI is, again, more sensitive to changes in low than high concentration values of CNDs. In equal concentration in SW of 200 ppm, CNDs outperform GS8 in medium to high permeability samples (R120 and R516) and yield comparable recovery to GS8 in the low permeability sample R015 (Fig. 7a–c). In very dilute concentration of CNDs in the SW, the 20 folds more concentrated GS8 outperforms CNDs by only 4.6–5.3 recovery points (Fig. 7g–i).

A general conclusion about the terminal Stage in the pattern flooding experiments is that all patterns resulted in comparable or identical total recovery values. The main exception to this conclusion is R516, the high permeability reservoir sample, tested with CNDs concentration at 50 and 200 ppm (Fig. 7a and d). In both cases, SW-GS8-CND produced higher total recovery values than SW-CND-GS8. Moreover, SW-{CND,GS8} yielded matching ultimate-recovery to SW-CND-GS8 in all samples. More interestingly, from mixed additives flooding, the recovery mechanism presents a step-wise convoluted response of CNDs (early influencer) and GS8 (delayed effect). The terminal Stage in SW-{CND,GS8} seems to partially track the initial path (in onset and reach) of CNDs in Stage 2. This could be attributed to the slight increase of the oil–water IFT with the CNDs/GS8 mixture over GS8 alone (Table 5). Accordingly, the combined additives contribution on recovery seems to favor an initial dynamic wettability alteration (similar to CNDs) over IFT reduction (mainly attributed to GS8).

We monitored the dynamics of the saturation profile in situ using an X-ray coreflood setup. The average fluid phase distribution at each section along the core was measured and used to characterize the two-phase (unsteady state) relative permeability of oil and seawater (with or without additives). We employed a long outcrop Indiana limestone sample (1.5” x 6”) in these tests. The sample was aged at room temperature for one week and at 100 °C for 1-day. Two sequential (SW-CND-GS8 and SW-GS8-CND) and one continuous (CNDs) flooding experiments were performed. The concentration of CNDs and GS8 in the SW was maintained at 200 ppm wherever applicable in these tests.

Fig. 8 shows the dynamics of the water saturation profile at selected injection volumes in a SW-CND-GS8 pattern (Fig. 8a corresponding to Test #39 in Table 3) and a SW-GS8-CND pattern (Fig. 8b corresponding to Test #40 in Table 3). The black solid response curve at the bottom of the graph in these figures is the baseline saturation profile at the irreducible connate water saturation,
S
wi
. We scanned samples every 0.1 PV of injected fluid (i.e., ten times per injected pore volume). Scanning was performed in-situ without interrupting the injection of fluids. Each scan takes about 3–4 min to complete. The injected volume of fluids during the scan is small compared to the total pore volume of the sample. Accordingly, it is safe to assume that the variation in the saturation profile during the scan is also negligible. For clarity, we opted to show responses only at selected injection pore volumes. The neat SW coreflood (Stage 1) is represented with saturation profiles at 0, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, and 3.4 PV. At 3.4 PV, we confirmed the recovery plateau with an invariant saturation profile for more than 1 PV of SW injection. Stage 2 in the sequential pattern is represented with saturation profiles at ＋1.5, ＋3.0, and ＋4.5 PV to better visualize the effect of CNDs (Fig. 8a) and GS8 (Fig. 8b) on the recovery rate. Stage 2 testing involved injecting a total of 4.5 PV of the additive solution in the SW. In Fig. 8, Sor1, Sor2, and Sor3 are the residual oil saturation (
S
or
= 1
–
S
w
) at the end of Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3, respectively.

Due to capillary end effects, the sample demonstrated water saturation profiles with an increasing trend following oil injection (0 PV SW) and decreasing trends during SW injection (0.2–3.4 PV SW). This can be attributed to a capillary discontinuity from inlet to outlet. It is also influenced by the dynamics of fluid flow at the inlet and the friction forces along the sample in approaching the outlet. In a piston-drive flood with one fluid displacing another, the inlet face is subjected to a higher PV of the displacing fluid than the outlet face. Ideally, the flooding volume of a (thin) cross-section of the sample decreases as the location of the section is further away from the inlet.

Fig. 8 shows that the saturation profile for both CNDs and GS8 is more stable and uniform than the ones observed during the SW flood. The end effects are almost absent in both cases, with the exception of a small drop at the inlet side.

It is possible to adjust the capillary number to reduce this end effect. The capillary discontinuity is reduced by injecting fluid with a higher capillary number (Andersen and Zhou, 2020). This contributes to the reduction of the oil–water IFT using, for example, surfactants. The capillary numbers for the CNDs and GS8 at 200 ppm in the SW were calculated at 4.44 × 10−8 and 2.40 × 10−7, respectively. Both capillary numbers are higher than that of the neat seawater (3.72 × 10−8). Despite a minor contribution in increasing the capillary number from the CNDs (19%) in comparison with the GS8 (545%) over the neat SW, the impact on the saturation profile from both additives is very comparable.

Wettability impacts oil recovery. A favorable dynamic wettability alteration also affects the relative permeability and the capillary pressure in oil-wet regions. This reduces the capillary end effect and increases oil production. The density of the injection fluids (SW, CNDs in SW, and GS8 in SW) remained constant during testing. And reversing the flow in the sample yielded a similar response at all stages of the coreflood testing (Fig. 8). Hence, the interpretation of the X-ray saturation profiles is believed to be factual.

Capillary end effects are often mitigated by increasing the velocity or the viscosity of the injection fluid (Guédon et al., 2017). They can also be mitigated by lowering the IFT, as discussed earlier. In this study, we targeted a low injection rate of 0.1 cc/min that mimics the waterflood front movement in the field (1 to 2 ft/day). Both CNDs and GS8 in dilute concentrations in the SW realized the targeted oil recovery enhancement with no end effects.

Fig. 9 presents the saturation profiles for a direct CNDs in Sw injection on the oil-saturated and aged sample. The test omits the neat SW flood (Test #41, Table 3) of the previous cases. Direct injection of CNDs resulted in less severe end effects of neat SW flood due to the improved wetting character of the CNDs. Unlike neat SW, CNDs (and GS8) in SW generated better saturation profile characteristics and uniform sweep of the rock sample.

Fig. 10 presents the X-ray Coreflood oil recovery (%OOIP) versus the fluid injection (PV) for the different flood patterns (SW-CND-GS8, SW-GS8-CND, and CND) on the same sample, S065. Each point on the plot averages a profile response over the length of the sample. The overall recovery performance for SW-GS8-CND (71.1%) is slightly better than that of SW-CND-GS8 (69.2%). This could be attributed to a better apparent performance in Stage 1 (SW flood) of the SW-GS8-CND pattern. CNDs and GS8 performed comparably in Stages 2 and 3 (see also Sections 3.1.1 and 3.1.2). The ORI for CNDs and GS8 is measured at 22.19% and 22.88% in Stage 2 and 9% and 10.11% in Stage 3, respectively. And as stated previously, GS8 generated a faster reach than CNDs with earlier onset of recovery. On the other hand, the overall recovery performance for direct CND injection nearly matched that at the end of Stage 2 in the SW-CND-GS8 pattern flood with a value of 58.3%.

Figs. 11 and 12 show the estimated unsteady state two-phase relative permeability plots for all experiments (Tests #39–41 of Table 3) of the X-ray coreflood study. These plots are derived using the power-law model of Eqs. (3) and (4). Figs. 11a and b pertain to SW-CND-GS8 (Test #39 of Table 3) and SW-GS8-CND (Test #40 of Table 3), respectively.

Initially, we derived the relative permeability curves for the sequential flooding for CNDs and GS8 after seawater flood. Fig. 11a and b present the unsteady-state relative permeability curves of SW-CND-GS8 and SW-GS8-CND flooding patterns, respectively. Each stage in each pattern is represented with one pair of
K
rw
and
K
ro
curves. In Figs. 11 and 12,
K
ro
(CND), for example, denotes the oil relative permeability in the stage involving CNDs injection. In Fig. 11a, the crossover saturation points are 28, 59, and 70% for the sequential injection of SW, CND, and GS8, respectively. The numbers for the SW-GS8-CND pattern are marked at 26, 57, and 67% for the SW, GS8, and CNDs injections. A crossover saturation point exceeding 50% is indicative of water-wetness. The SW flood in both patterns of Fig. 11 demonstrates less than 50% crossover point (strongly oil-wet state). The numbers improve favorably with the sequential injection of both CNDs in SW and GS8 in SW.

Similarly, we derived the relative permeability curves for the direct CND flood (Test #41 of Table 3). Fig. 12 shows the comparison of the relative permeability characteristics for the SW flood (Test #39 of Table 3) and the direct CND flood (Test #41 of Table 3). The same figure also shows a clear difference in the flow dynamics of oil and water with respect to SW and CNDs injections. The neat SW flood exhibited an early crossover point at 27% with a maximum saturation of 46%. On the other hand, the direct CND flood demonstrated a larger crossover point at 38% and a maximum saturation point at 66%. CNDs altered the dynamic wettability more favorably from oil-wet to weakly water-wet and resulted in improved water saturation.

It is worth noting from Fig. 12 that at any given water saturation,
K
ro
(CND) is larger than
K
ro
(SW) and
K
rw
(CND) is smaller than
K
rw
(SW). This indicates that CNDs are more efficient than neat SW in altering the dynamic wettability of the oil-wet rock and mobilizing the oil. Kanj et al. 2020 attributed the dynamic wettability alteration of CNDs to an efficient wedging mechanism at the three-phase contact line that diminishes the capillary pressure and enhances the disjoining pressure. This means that CNDs facilitate capillary pressure reduction in the non-wetting fluid (oil) and contribute to the additional flow of the wetting nanofluid (water) into the wedge. As such, CNDs increase oil mobility and decrease water mobility. In the original oil-wet state of the core, SW reaches the well-connected larger pores and leaves behind the sizeable residual or bypassed oil (probably at the meso- and micropores) due to their high capillarity, which results in increased water relative permeability,
K
rw
(SW), and decreased oil relative permeability,
K
ro
(SW).

To further understand the recovery mechanisms with CNDs and GS8 flooding, we measured the static and the dynamic wettability (contact angle) on outcrop (Indianan limestone) and carbonate reservoir (Arab-D) samples in a rock–oil–water system. Samples were treated with crude oil for two weeks to restore the rock’s wetting behavior, as described in Kanj et al. (2020).

Fig. 13 shows the effect of CNDs and GS8 on the static wettability alteration of both outcrop and reservoir samples at 15%
S
wi
in Formation Water. Both CNDs and GS8 transitioned the surface wettability from strongly oil-wet to intermediate wet. Tests were repeated for varying levels of
S
wi
with comparable results. CNDs and GS8 impacted the IFT differently (as indicated earlier) and accordingly influenced the shape of the drop differently as well.

Fig. 14 shows the dynamic wettability alteration by displacing the base fluid (SW) with either SW or 200 ppm CNDs in SW. Tests were done on outcrop and reservoir samples as a function of the irreducible water saturation in the sample,
S
wi
. Two
S
wi
values were considered, 0 and 15%. SW demonstrated only minor (3%–4%) dynamic wettability alteration effects on fully oil-saturated samples (
S
wi
= 0
). When
S
wi
is increased to 15%, the change in dynamic wettability was more favorable for both outcrop and reservoir samples (open markers versus filled markers in Fig. 14). Moreover, CNDs in SW demonstrated a strong influence on the dynamic wettability compared to neat SW (red lines versus blue lines in Fig. 14). Overall, the increase in
S
wi
created a clear and positive wettability shift towards water-wetness. And the reservoir sample demonstrated stronger oil-wetness tendencies than the outcrop sample, though initially, the oil-wetness favored the outcrop sample following a static wettability testing (t = 0). The outcrop (Indiana Limestone) and the reservoir (Arab-D) samples originate from two different rock formations. The difference in contact angle effect can be attributed to their native wettabilities. The reservoir rock is inherently more oil-wet. In addition, the two rock samples exhibit only a slight variation in permeability and surface roughness. Overall, the observed difference in the CND wettability alteration effect on the two rocks is less than 6%.

Table 5 presents the oil–seawater IFT measurements with and without additives in the SW. Measurements are made at room and reservoir temperature. Lowering IFT reduces oil adherence to the rock and improves water spreading. GS8 demonstrates a larger influence on IFT than CNDs. At 200 ppm in SW, GS8 contributes to an 83% reduction in IFT. It takes more than 25 times this concentration in the SW for CNDs to generate the same effect. Wasan and Nikolov (2003) described that the nanoparticles in the aqueous phase self-assemble in the wedge and induce a disjoining pressure at the three-phase contact line (Wasan and Nikolov, 2003). Kanj et al. (2020) also visualized the spreading efficiency and oil sweeping capability of CNDs on oil-coated glass plates (Kanj et al., 2020). The recovery with CNDs is most likely dominated by its increased wetting characteristics of rock surfaces. On the other hand, the recovery with GS8 is driven by a reduction of interfacial forces between the oil and water systems. This reduces the high capillarity of the residual oil and enables its emulsification and mobilization. However, the combined effect of CNDs and GS8 showed no synergism on the recovery. The combination of CNDs with GS8 increases the oil–water IFT slightly, as seen in Table 5. This IFT increase is caused by the competitive arrangement of the two additives and their interactions at the oil–water​ interface.

In the past, we field-tested CNDs as passive tracers. We successfully demonstrated its colloidal stability, resistance to adsorption, fast mobility, high detectability to ppb levels, low-cost synthesis, and ease of scalability. We also studied its wettability alteration and spontaneous imbibition for active recovery support. The current study examined the CNDs EOR efficiency in dilute concentrations (i.e., 10–200 ppm) for continuous use in the injection seawater. We benchmarked the CNDs performance against a well-tested in-house Gemini surfactant, GS8, with exceptional surfactancy properties that are well suited for the harsh carbonate reservoir conditions. Comparing the performance of CNDs and GS8 at equal dilute concentrations in the SW, GS8 caused a faster onset of recovery and a shorter reach or a higher rate of recovery than CNDs. CNDs contributed, however, to a higher plateau despite the rapid early positive impact of GS8. The added oil recovery with CNDs in SW and GS8 in SW ranged from 3%–23% and 6%–22%, respectively.

A HP/HT X-ray coreflood setup helped monitor the 1-D saturation profile on one long outcrop sample in two sequential (SW-CND-GS8 and SW-GS8-CNDs) and one direct (CNDs) pattern floods. We observed severe end effects with the neat sweater injection in the X-ray coreflood experiments. The 1-D saturation profiles exhibited increasing trends from inlet to outlet following oil injection and decreasing trends during the following SW flood. This is attributed to capillary discontinuity and frictional forces along the sample. Both additives increased the SW capillary number, and accordingly, contributed to reducing the capillary discontinuity with no end effects.

Numerically derived relative permeability curves provided qualitative interpretation of the dominant mechanisms in SW, CNDs in SW, and GS8 in SW floods. The use of CNDs and GS8 had shown a clear advantage on the flow characteristics of oil due to their improved wetting nature. Thus, the relative permeability of the oil (kro) increased, and the relative permeability of the water (krw) decreased with the injection of either CNDs or GS8 in the SW. Measurements of static and dynamic wettability of the rock–oil–brine system revealed that the addition of CNDs and GS8 had significantly altered the wettability towards the water-wet state, more so statically than dynamically. An increase of Swi supported a larger positive shift in the dynamic contact angle. On the other hand, we also measured the oil–water interfacial tension (IFT) with and without additives. Unlike GS8, CNDs demonstrated only a minor effect on IFT and a stronger impact on dynamic wettability alteration.

The advantage of CNDs is not only in its ease of synthesis and scalability but also in its total recovery efficiency at very dilute concentrations in the injection water. The results reinforce the deep-reservoir EOR vision with CNDs and possibly undermine the need for very closely spaced wells in EOR pilots in the field.

Sivabalan Sakthivel: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xianmin Zhou: Methodology. Emmanuel P. Giannelis: Methodology, CNDs concept design & synthesis, Writing – review & editing. Mazen Y. Kanj: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – results and discussions section, Writing – original draft.
